title: Maharashtrian Amti Dal

ingredients.name: kokum, dried
visual_checks: the key souring agent in Maharashtrian cooking; substitute tamarind if unavailable
ingredients.notes: the key souring agent in Maharashtrian cooking; substitute tamarind if unavailable

subs.name: 1.5 tbsp tamarind pulp; add after the dal comes to a boil

ingredients.name: jaggery
visual_checks: essential — amti is always slightly sweet
ingredients.notes: essential — amti is always slightly sweet

subs.name: 1.5 tsp sugar; the sweetness will be cleaner but less complex

ingredients.name: goda masala (Maharashtrian garam masala)
visual_checks: goda masala has a sweeter, coconut-and-star-anise flavour; regular garam masala is an acceptable substitute
ingredients.notes: goda masala has a sweeter, coconut-and-star-anise flavour; regular garam masala is an acceptable substitute

subs.name: use regular garam masala + 1 tsp coriander powder + a tiny pinch of star anise powder to approximate goda masala

ingredients.name: fresh coconut, grated
visual_checks: added at the end gives richness; skip for a plainer amti
ingredients.notes: added at the end gives richness; skip for a plainer amti

steps.text: Rinse toor dal and soak for 20 minutes. Pressure-cook with 2.5 cups water, turmeric and a pinch of salt for 4 whistles on medium. Open once the pressure drops naturally and whisk the cooked dal vigorously until smooth and creamy.

Heat oil in a saucepan on medium-high. Add mustard seeds; when they pop add cumin, asafoetida, dried red chillies and curry leaves. Fry for 20 seconds until the leaves crackle and the chillies darken slightly.

Pour the tempered oil directly into the cooked dal in the pressure cooker (or transfer the dal to the saucepan). Add kokum, jaggery, red chilli powder, coriander powder and goda masala. Stir well and bring to a gentle boil.

Add 0.5 cup water if the dal is very thick — amti should be pourable like a thin soup, not a paste. Simmer on medium for 8 minutes so the kokum infuses its colour and sourness and the jaggery dissolves completely. Taste and adjust the sweet-sour-spice balance — this is the defining moment of the dish.
Stir in grated coconut (if using) and cook for 1 more minute. Scatter coriander leaves and serve hot over steamed rice with a side of Maharashtrian bhaji, papad and koshimbir (raita).

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Follow the cooker manufacturer's fill, venting and opening instructions; never force the lid open under pressure.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: kokum → 1.5 tbsp tamarind pulp; add after the dal comes to a boil; garam-masala → use regular garam masala + 1 tsp coriander powder + a tiny pinch of star anise powder to approximate goda masala; jaggery → 1.5 tsp sugar; the sweetness will be cleaner but less complex
